content:
# Luke Chapter 23 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 117-119 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_023_page_001.jpg, LUK_023_page_002.jpg, LUK_023_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And the whole assembly of them rose up and brought him before Pilate.

**2** And they began to accuse him, saying, "We found this man perverting our nation, and forbidding the giving of tribute to Caesar, and saying that he himself is Christ, a king."

**3** And Pilate asked him, saying, "Are you the King of the Jews?" And he answered him and said, "You say so."

**4** And Pilate said to the chief priests and the crowds, "I find no guilt in this man."

**5** But they grew more insistent, saying, "He stirs up the people, teaching throughout all Judea, beginning from Galilee even to this place."

**6** And when Pilate heard this, he asked whether the man was a Galilean.

**7** And when he learned that he was from the jurisdiction of Herod, he sent him to Herod, who was himself also in Jerusalem in those days.

**8** And Herod, when he saw Jesus, was exceedingly glad, for he had been wanting to see him for a long time, because he had heard about him, and he was hoping to see some sign performed by him.

**9** And he questioned him with many words, but he answered him nothing.

**10** And the chief priests and the scribes stood by, vehemently accusing him.

**11** And Herod with his soldiers, having treated him with contempt and mocked him, dressing him in splendid clothing, sent him back to Pilate.

**12** And Herod and Pilate became friends with one another on that very day, for previously they had been at enmity with each other.

**13** And Pilate, having called together the chief priests and the rulers and the people,

**14** said to them, "You brought this man to me as one who is turning the people away, and behold, I, having examined him before you, found no guilt in this man concerning those things of which you accuse him.

**15** No, nor did Herod, for he sent him back to us; and behold, nothing worthy of death has been done by him.

**16** Therefore, having disciplined him, I will release him."

**17** [Now he was obliged to release one to them at the feast.]

**18** But they cried out all together, saying, "Away with this man, and release to us Barabbas!" --

**19** who had been thrown into prison for a certain insurrection that had taken place in the city, and for murder.

**20** And Pilate again addressed them, desiring to release Jesus.

**21** But they kept shouting, saying, "Crucify, crucify him!"

**22** And he said to them a third time, "Why? What evil has this man done? I have found no cause for death in him. Therefore, having disciplined him, I will release him."

**23** But they were insistent with loud voices, demanding that he be crucified; and their voices prevailed.

**24** And Pilate decided that their demand should be granted.

**25** And he released the one who had been thrown into prison for insurrection and murder, whom they were asking for, but Jesus he delivered over to their will.

**26** And as they led him away, they seized Simon, a certain Cyrenian, coming from the country, and laid upon him the cross, to carry it behind Jesus.

**27** And there followed him a great multitude of the people, and of women who were mourning and lamenting for him.

**28** But Jesus, turning to them, said, "Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and for your children.

**29** For behold, the days are coming in which they will say, 'Blessed are the barren, and the wombs that never bore, and the breasts that never nursed.'

**30** Then they will begin to say to the mountains, 'Fall on us,' and to the hills, 'Cover us.'

**31** For if they do these things in the green wood, what will happen in the dry?"

**32** And two others also, who were criminals, were led with him to be put to death.

**33** And when they came to the place called The Skull, there they crucified him, and the criminals -- one on the right and one on the left.

**34** [And Jesus said, "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing."] And dividing his garments, they cast lots.

**35** And the people stood watching. And the rulers also sneered at him, saying, "He saved others; let him save himself, if this is the Christ of God, the Chosen One."

**36** And the soldiers also mocked him, coming to him and offering him vinegar,

**37** and saying, "If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself."

**38** And there was also an inscription over him: "This is the King of the Jews."

**39** And one of the criminals who were hanged was blaspheming him, saying, "Are you not the Christ? Save yourself and us!"

**40** But the other, answering, rebuked him, saying, "Do you not even fear God, since you are under the same condemnation?

**41** And we indeed justly, for we are receiving what is worthy of the things we have done; but this man has done nothing wrong."

**42** And he said, "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom."

**43** And he said to him, "Truly I say to you, today you will be with me in Paradise."

**44** And it was already about the sixth hour, and darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour,

**45** the sun's light failing; and the curtain of the temple was torn in the middle.

**46** And Jesus, crying out with a loud voice, said, "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit." And having said this, he breathed his last.

**47** And the centurion, having seen what had taken place, glorified God, saying, "Certainly this man was righteous."

**48** And all the crowds who had gathered together for this spectacle, having observed the things that took place, returned home beating their breasts.

**49** And all those known to him stood at a distance, and the women who had followed him from Galilee, seeing these things.

**50** And behold, a man named Joseph, who was a member of the council, a good and righteous man --

**51** he had not consented to their plan and action -- from Arimathea, a city of the Jews, who was himself waiting for the kingdom of God:

**52** this man went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus.

**53** And he took it down and wrapped it in linen cloth, and laid him in a tomb hewn in stone, where no one had ever yet been laid.

**54** And it was the day of Preparation, and the Sabbath was drawing near.

**55** And the women who had come with him from Galilee followed after, and they saw the tomb and how his body was laid.

**56** And they returned and prepared spices and ointments. And on the Sabbath they rested according to the commandment.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

I examined all three pages of the Codex Sinaiticus facsimile (PDF pages 117-119) for Luke 23. The text begins in the rightmost column of page 117 (labeled Lk. 23:7 at bottom) and continues through page 118 into page 119 (concluding around the second column before the transition to Luke 24). The manuscript is written in scriptio continua across four columns per page in the characteristic Sinaiticus majuscule hand.

**Critical Variant -- Verse 34 (the prayer of forgiveness):** On page 118 (third column, around Lk. 23:32-34), I examined the area where the crucifixion narrative and the disputed prayer appear. The text flows continuously through this section. The words of the prayer are present in the manuscript. There is scholarly disagreement about whether the original scribe (Scribe A) included this verse or whether it was added by a corrector. EMET reports the text is present and "integrated into the flow" without obvious signs of insertion. PNEUMA states more strongly that the original scribe omitted it and a corrector added it. LOGOS takes a middle position. The manuscript image at this resolution does not allow me to definitively adjudicate between scribal hands. What is clear is: (1) the text IS present in Sinaiticus as it stands; (2) the verse is ABSENT from P75 and the original hand of Vaticanus (B); (3) the omission is arguably the harder reading. I have retained the verse in brackets following LOGOS's approach, which represents the wisest balance between fidelity to the Sinaiticus tradition and honesty about the textual uncertainty. See the detailed note below.

### Translation Principles Applied

The SOPHIA consensus for this chapter follows the formal register established by LOGOS as the base, since LOGOS consistently achieved the best balance between accuracy and readability. EMET's literalism was drawn upon wherever it preserved important Greek structures (participial constructions, word order emphasis). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings were adopted selectively where they genuinely improved clarity without sacrificing precision. The paratactic "And" (kai) that opens most verses was preserved, following both EMET and LOGOS, as it is characteristic of Luke's narrative style in the Passion account.

### Verse-by-Verse Divergence Notes

**Verse 1** -- Minor divergence. EMET: "having risen-up, the whole multitude of them led him before Pilate." LOGOS: "the whole assembly of them rose up and brought him before Pilate." PNEUMA: "the whole assembly rose and brought him before Pilate." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA's "assembly" (more natural English for plethos in this judicial context) and "rose up and brought" (clearer than EMET's participial chain). All three agree on the core meaning.

**Verse 2** -- EMET: "perverting our nation." LOGOS: "perverting our nation." PNEUMA: "undermining our nation." SOPHIA retains "perverting" with EMET and LOGOS, as diastrephonta literally means "turning aside/perverting" and this is a formal accusation before a Roman governor -- the legal register is appropriate. PNEUMA's "undermining" is interpretive. PNEUMA also renders "Christ" as "Messiah" -- SOPHIA retains "Christ" as a transliteration of the Greek Christos that Sinaiticus reads.

**Verse 3** -- EMET: "You say [it]." LOGOS: "You say so." PNEUMA: "Those are your words." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "You say so" as the most faithful and natural rendering of su legeis. PNEUMA's expansion ("Those are your words") adds interpretive weight not in the Greek.

**Verse 4** -- EMET: "no cause." LOGOS: "no guilt." PNEUMA: "no basis for a charge." SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "no guilt" (aition means "cause/guilt/ground for accusation"). "Guilt" is concise and captures the judicial sense. PNEUMA's "no basis for a charge" is accurate but overly paraphrastic.

**Verse 5** -- EMET: "they were insisting." LOGOS: "they grew more insistent." PNEUMA: "they pressed harder." SOPHIA follows LOGOS -- "grew more insistent" captures the imperfect tense (ongoing escalation) of epischyon well. PNEUMA's "pressed harder" is vivid but less precise.

**Verse 8** -- EMET: "rejoiced exceedingly." LOGOS: "was exceedingly glad." PNEUMA: "was thrilled." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "thrilled" is too colloquial for the register of this translation. All three correctly note Herod's desire to see a sign.

**Verse 9** -- EMET: "he questioned him with many words; but he answered nothing to him." LOGOS: "he questioned him with many words, but he answered him nothing." PNEUMA: "He peppered Jesus with question after question, but Jesus gave him no answer at all." SOPHIA follows LOGOS for register consistency. PNEUMA's "peppered" is vivid but too informal.

**Verse 11** -- All three agree on "splendid clothing" (EMET/LOGOS) or "magnificent robe" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA uses "splendid clothing" with the majority. PNEUMA's "made a spectacle of him" for exouthenesas is interpretive; SOPHIA retains "treated him with contempt" with EMET and LOGOS.

**Verse 14** -- EMET: "no cause." LOGOS: "no guilt." PNEUMA: "no grounds for the charges." SOPHIA follows LOGOS for consistency with verse 4.

**Verse 15** -- EMET: "he sent-back him to us." LOGOS: "he sent him back to us." PNEUMA: "he sent him back to us." All agree. EMET begins with "but neither [did] Herod" while LOGOS has "No, nor did Herod" -- SOPHIA follows LOGOS for clarity.

**Verse 16** -- EMET: "Having disciplined him." LOGOS: "having disciplined him." PNEUMA: "I will have him flogged." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS in retaining "disciplined" (paideusas), which is the literal meaning. PNEUMA's "flogged" is an interpretive specification -- the Greek verb can mean various forms of correction/punishment.

**Verse 17** -- All three bracket this verse as textually uncertain. SOPHIA retains brackets. EMET: "he had necessity to release." LOGOS: "he was obliged to release." PNEUMA: "He was obligated to release." SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

**Verse 18** -- EMET: "Take-away this [one]." LOGOS: "Away with this man." PNEUMA: "Away with this man!" SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA's "Away with this man" -- more natural English for aire touton.

**Verse 20** -- EMET: "called-out to them, wanting to release Jesus." LOGOS: "again addressed them, desiring to release Jesus." PNEUMA: "wanting to release Jesus, appealed to them again." SOPHIA follows LOGOS for word order and register.

**Verse 22** -- EMET: "For what evil did this [one] do?" LOGOS: "Why? What evil has this man done?" PNEUMA: "Why? What crime has this man committed?" SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "crime" is more interpretive than kakon ("evil").

**Verse 23** -- EMET: "their voices were prevailing." LOGOS: "their voices prevailed." PNEUMA: "their voices won out." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. All three capture katischyon accurately; PNEUMA's "won out" is colloquial.

**Verse 24** -- EMET: "decided [for] their demand to come-about." LOGOS: "decided that their demand should be granted." PNEUMA: "handed down the verdict they wanted." SOPHIA follows LOGOS for clarity and fidelity. PNEUMA's "handed down the verdict" over-interprets epekrinen.

**Verse 25** -- EMET: "handed-over to their will." LOGOS: "delivered up to their will." PNEUMA: "surrendered Jesus to their will." SOPHIA uses "delivered over" -- combining the strength of LOGOS's "delivered" with a slight adjustment. All three convey paredoken accurately.

**Verse 26** -- EMET: "having seized Simon, a certain Cyrenian, coming from [the] field." LOGOS: "they seized Simon, a certain Cyrenian, coming from the country." PNEUMA: "they seized a man from Cyrene named Simon, who was coming in from the countryside." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA reorders to put "from Cyrene" before the name, which is natural but departs from the Greek word order.

**Verse 27** -- EMET: "beating [their breasts] and lamenting him." LOGOS: "mourning and lamenting for him." PNEUMA: "beating their chests and wailing for him." The Greek koptomai means "to beat the breast / mourn." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "mourning and lamenting" as the more dignified rendering that captures both physical and emotional dimensions.

**Verse 29** -- Minor variations in wording; all three agree in substance. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

**Verse 31** -- EMET: "if in the green wood they do these-things, in the dry what might happen?" LOGOS: "if they do these things in the green wood, what will happen in the dry?" PNEUMA: "if they do these things when the wood is green, what will happen when it is dry?" SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's temporal rendering ("when the wood is green") is interpretive -- the Greek uses locative en, "in," not a temporal construction.

**Verse 32** -- EMET: "two others, criminals, were being led." LOGOS: "two others also, who were criminals, were led." PNEUMA: "Two others -- both criminals -- were also led out." The Greek heteroi duo kakourgoi is syntactically ambiguous -- it could mean "two other criminals" (implying Jesus is one) or "two others, [who were] criminals." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's rendering "two others also, who were criminals" as it preserves the distinction that the criminals are a separate category from Jesus.

**Verse 33** -- All three substantially agree. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

**Verse 34** -- THE CRITICAL VARIANT. This is the most significant textual decision in the chapter.

EMET includes the verse WITHOUT brackets, arguing that Sinaiticus reads it in the main hand and the text is integrated without signs of insertion.

LOGOS includes the verse IN BRACKETS [ ], acknowledging the textual uncertainty while preserving the reading.

PNEUMA includes the verse with HALF-BRACKETS, stating that the original scribe omitted it and a corrector added it.

SOPHIA's decision: The verse is retained IN BRACKETS, following LOGOS. The evidence: (1) The verse IS present in Sinaiticus as it stands today. (2) It is ABSENT from P75 and the original hand of Vaticanus -- two of the earliest and most reliable witnesses. (3) The omission is the harder reading: it is easy to imagine a scribe adding a prayer of forgiveness from the cross, but difficult to explain why anyone would remove it. (4) Some early Fathers (notably Origen) knew the verse but others did not cite it. The brackets signal to the reader that this beloved saying, while deeply embedded in the Christian tradition, has genuine textual uncertainty. EMET's unbracketed approach is too confident given the external evidence. PNEUMA's half-brackets are a reasonable alternative but non-standard.

**Verse 35** -- EMET/LOGOS: "the Christ of God, the Chosen One." PNEUMA: "God's Messiah, the Chosen One." SOPHIA retains "the Christ of God, the Chosen One" -- keeping the Greek title Christos transliterated rather than translated to "Messiah." The Sinaiticus reading ho eklektos ("the Chosen One") is confirmed across all three translators.

**Verse 38** -- EMET: "The king of the Jews, this [one]" (preserving the Sinaiticus word order with houtos at end). LOGOS: "This is the King of the Jews." PNEUMA: "THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS." The Sinaiticus reads O BASILEUS TWN IOUDAIWN OUTOS, with houtos ("this one") placed at the end. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's rendering "This is the King of the Jews" as standard English, but notes in council that EMET's word order is closer to the manuscript. All three correctly note the absence of the "in Greek and Latin and Hebrew" expansion found in later manuscripts.

**Verse 39** -- EMET: "hanging criminals was blaspheming him." LOGOS: "criminals who were hanged was blaspheming him." PNEUMA: "criminals hanging there hurled insults at him." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Greek kremasthenton ("having been hanged/suspended") is a participle referring to crucifixion. "Blaspheming" (blasphemeo) is retained over PNEUMA's "hurled insults" as it carries the theological weight Luke intends.

**Verse 40** -- EMET: "you are in the same judgment." LOGOS: "you are under the same condemnation." PNEUMA: "You're under the same sentence he is." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Condemnation" (krima) is more precise than EMET's generic "judgment." PNEUMA adds "he is" which is not in the Greek.

**Verse 41** -- EMET: "did nothing out-of-place." LOGOS: "has done nothing wrong." PNEUMA: "has done nothing wrong." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA -- "nothing wrong" is a clear rendering of ouden atopon ("nothing out of place/improper"). EMET's hyper-literal "out-of-place" preserves the etymology but obscures the meaning.

**Verse 43** -- EMET: "Amen, I say to you." LOGOS: "Truly I say to you." PNEUMA: "I tell you the truth." SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "Truly I say to you" -- this preserves the solemn formula while translating amen into English. EMET's transliterated "Amen" is defensible but inconsistent with rendering it elsewhere. PNEUMA's restructuring loses the formulaic quality.

**Verse 44** -- EMET/LOGOS retain "the sixth hour...the ninth hour." PNEUMA converts to "noon...three in the afternoon." SOPHIA retains the hour designations with EMET/LOGOS -- this is a translation from an ancient manuscript, and the temporal markers are part of the text's character.

**Verse 45** -- EMET: "the sun having failed." LOGOS: "the sun's light failing." PNEUMA: "the sun's light failed." All three correctly identify the Sinaiticus reading eklipontos (from ekleipo, "to fail/cease") rather than the variant eskotisthe ("was darkened"). SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "the sun's light failing" -- LOGOS's addition of "light" makes the genitive absolute clearer in English while preserving the participial force. EMET: "the veil of the temple was split." LOGOS: "the curtain of the temple was torn." PNEUMA: "The curtain of the temple was torn." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "curtain" (more accessible than "veil" for katapetasma) and "torn" (more natural than "split" for eschisthe).

**Verse 46** -- EMET: "I commit my spirit." LOGOS: "I commit my spirit." PNEUMA: "I place my spirit." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "commit" (paratithemai carries the sense of entrusting/depositing, and "commit" has both the weight of trust and the finality appropriate to the moment). EMET: "he expired." LOGOS: "he breathed his last." PNEUMA: "he breathed his last." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA -- "breathed his last" (exepneusen) is both literal and natural.

**Verse 47** -- All three agree on "righteous" for dikaios -- a critical Lukan distinctive. EMET: "Certainly this man was righteous." LOGOS: "Certainly this man was righteous." PNEUMA: "There is no doubt -- this was a righteous man." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS for word order, which matches the Greek emphasis (ontos ho anthropos houtos dikaios en).

**Verse 48** -- EMET: "were returning, beating [their] breasts." LOGOS: "returned home striking their breasts." PNEUMA: "went home beating their chests in grief." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Beating their breasts" and "striking their breasts" are equivalent; SOPHIA uses "beating" as slightly more common English idiom. PNEUMA's "in grief" is added interpretation.

**Verse 50** -- EMET: "a good and righteous man." LOGOS: "a good and righteous man." PNEUMA: "a good and upright man." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "righteous" (dikaios) -- the same word used for Jesus in v.47, and Luke's repetition is intentional and should be preserved.

**Verse 51** -- EMET: "was not having consented to the counsel and deed of them." LOGOS: "had not consented to their plan and action." PNEUMA: "had not agreed with the council's decision or their actions." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "council's decision" confusingly introduces "council" (the body) when the Greek boule means "plan/counsel."

**Verse 53** -- EMET: "a tomb hewn-in-rock." LOGOS: "a tomb hewn in stone." PNEUMA: "a rock-cut tomb." All three are valid renderings of laxeuto. SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "hewn in stone." The Sinaiticus reading is confirmed.

**Verse 54** -- EMET: "the Sabbath was dawning." LOGOS: "the Sabbath was drawing near." PNEUMA: "the Sabbath was about to begin." The Greek epephosken literally means "was dawning/beginning to shine," but since the Jewish Sabbath begins at sunset, "drawing near" (LOGOS) captures the meaning without the confusion "dawning" might create. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

**Verse 56** -- EMET: "spices and ointments." LOGOS: "spices and ointments." PNEUMA: "spices and perfumed oils." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. The Greek aromata kai myra is accurately rendered as "spices and ointments."

### Summary of Major Textual Decisions

| Verse | Issue | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| 17 | Disputed verse (Barabbas custom) | Retained in brackets -- probable harmonization from Matt 27:15 / Mark 15:6 |
| 34 | Prayer of forgiveness | Retained in brackets -- present in Sinaiticus but absent from P75 and Vaticanus; omission is the harder reading |
| 38 | Word order of inscription | Rendered in standard English order following LOGOS; Sinaiticus places houtos at end |
| 42 | "Into" vs. "in" your kingdom | "Into" (eis) following Sinaiticus reading, confirmed by all three translators |
| 45 | "Sun's light failing" vs. "sun darkened" | "Sun's light failing" following Sinaiticus eklipontos, confirmed by all three translators |
| 47 | "Righteous" vs. "innocent" | "Righteous" (dikaios) -- distinctly Lukan, confirmed by all three translators |

---
*Consensus translation produced by SOPHIA, 2026-03-05*
*In council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
*The SOPHIA Bible -- Wisdom through consilience*
*Codex Sinaiticus, 4th century CE, British Library*
